g.data-narration: Welcome to Digital Encoders. Today we explore how these combinational circuits convert multiple inputs into compact binary codes.
An encoder converts human-readable information into digital format. It has 2^n inputs and n outputs, producing binary code.
g.text: multiple input lines into a smaller set of output lines (binary code)

g.data-narration: There are two main types: Simple encoders handle one active input, while priority encoders resolve conflicts by prioritizing the highest input.
g.text: One active input at a time

g.data-narration: The 4-to-2 simple encoder truth table shows how each active input generates a unique 2-bit binary output code.
g.text: Each input maps to unique binary

g.data-narration: From the truth table, we derive Boolean expressions: Y1 equals I2 plus I3, and Y0 equals I1 plus I3, implemented with OR gates.
The circuit uses two OR gates to generate outputs. Input lines feed into gates based on the derived Boolean expressions.
Simple encoders have a critical flaw: multiple active inputs create ambiguous outputs, producing incorrect binary codes.
g.text: If inputs I₁=1 and I₂=1 simultaneously:
Assigns priority to inputs, highest priority wins

g.data-narration: A priority encoder assigns priority levels, with I3 having highest priority and I0 the lowest, resolving conflicts systematically.
g.text: at least one input is active

g.data-narration: The priority encoder truth table uses don't-care conditions (X) and includes a valid bit V to indicate when any input is active.
Priority encoder expressions are more complex: Y1 equals I2 or I3, Y0 uses I1 with conditions, and V is OR of all inputs.
A keyboard encoder demonstrates real-world application, converting key presses into ASCII codes using priority encoding for key rollover.
Encoders are essential in interrupt handling, analog-to-digital conversion, and data multiplexing, making them fundamental in digital systems.
Key points: Encoders convert multiple inputs to binary output, simple encoders need single active input, priority encoders resolve conflicts.
g.text: Function: Converts 2^n inputs → n binary outputs
Simple Encoder: Requires exactly ONE active input
Priority Encoder: Handles multiple inputs, highest wins
Valid Bit (V): Essential for distinguishing no-input state

g.data-narration: Encoders are fundamental building blocks in digital electronics, enabling efficient data handling across keyboards, interrupt systems, and converters.
g.text: Encoders are essential combinational circuits that enable efficient data handling
and reduce required connections in digital systems
